coredata.dc:title: Photoluminescence-tunable fluorescent carbon dots-deposited silver nanoparticle for detection and killing of bacteria
coredata.dc:creator: Roh, Sang Gyu
Robby, Akhmad Irhas
Phuong, Pham Thi My
In, Insik
Park, Sung Young
coredata.dc:description: Innovative methods to detect and kill pathogenic bacteria have a pivotal role in the eradication of infectious diseases and the prevention of the growth of antibiotic-resistant bacteria. The combination of fluorescent carbon dots (FCDs) with silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) is an effective material for synergic detection and antimicrobial activity determination. However, the fluorescence quenching of the FCDs owing to an interaction with AgNP is a major limitation. In this study, we designed a system to utilize poly(vinylpyrrolidone) (PVP) and catechol chemistry (PVP@Ag:FCD) in order to avoid the fluorescence quenching of the FCD-AgNP combination due to Forster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET). PVP@Ag:FCD exhibited bright fluorescence, which can be used for bacterial detection, through the promotion of electrostatic binding with the negatively-charged bacterial surface and generation of fluorescence quenching due to aggregation-induced quenching. Furthermore, the presence of silver nanoparticles in PVP@Ag:FCD produced an excellent bacteria killing efficiency against E. coli and S. aureus, even at low concentrations (0.1 mg/mL). In contaminated river water, the PVP@Ag:FCD system showed a simple, highly sensitive, and effective performance for both the detection and eradication of bacteria. Therefore, this system offers an auspicious method for the future detection and killing of bacteria.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent carbon dots
Silver nanoparticles
Catechol chemistry
Bacteria detection
Antibacterial activity

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast footnotes grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of catechol/ethyl bromide-quaternized PVP with AgNP (PVP@Ag)
Synthesis of catechol-crosslinked FCD with PVP@Ag (PVP@Ag:FCD)
Selectivity and anti-jamming test of PVP@Ag:FCD
Bacteria detection and antibacterial performance of PVP@Ag:FCD in “real-world” samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Ministry of Trade, Industry and Energy
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Trade, Industry and Energy

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by Grant No. 10062079 from the Ministry of Trade, Industry and Energy (MOTIE) and the Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education ( NRF-2017R1A2B2002365 and 2018R1A6A1A03023788 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Illustration of Ag-induced fluorescence quenching, (b) illustration of synthesis of PVP@Ag:FCD via catechol cross-linking for bacterial detection and killing.
(a) 1H NMR and (b) UV–visible spectra of PVP@Ag:FCD (0.1 mg/mL). Full-excitation wavelength PL spectra of (c) FCD and (d) PVP@Ag:FCD (1 mg/mL).
(a) Particle size distribution, (b) SEM-EDX, and (c) TEM analysis of FCD, PVP@Ag, and PVP@Ag:FCD.

Detection of E. coli and S. aureus (excitation wavelength of 400 nm) with various bacterial concentrations with fixed material concentration of 5 mg/mL (a, b) and various PVP@Ag:FCD concentrations with fixed bacterial concentration of 107 CFU/mL (c, d). The fluorescence changes were also observed using confocal images (e).
(a) SEM images of bacteria before and after treatment with PVP@Ag:FCD. (b) Cyclic voltammograms and (c) flow cytometry analysis of PVP@Ag:FCD before and after binding to E. coli and S. aureus.

Photographs of E. coli and S. aureus colony (107 CFU/mL) treated with (a) PVP@Ag and FCD (10 mg/mL), and (b) PVP@Ag:FCD in different concentrations. (c) Bacterial killing efficiency depends on PVP@Ag:FCD concentration. (d) Flow cytometry analysis of live and dead bacteria using SYTO 9 and PI staining. All experiments were conducted at 37 °C for 24 h.

Confocal images for bacteria detection using (a) distilled water (DDW) as a control, and (b) contaminated water from river treated with 5 mg/mL of PVP@Ag:FCD. Incubation conditions were at 37 °C for 24 h.

head.ce:title: Photoluminescence-tunable fluorescent carbon dots-deposited silver nanoparticle for detection and killing of bacteria

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Korea National University of Transportation, Chungju 380-702, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of IT Convergence, Korea National University of Transportation, Chungju 380-702, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Polymer Science and Engineering, Korea National University of Transportation, Chungju 380-702, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Polymer Science and Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Korea National University of Transportation, Chungju 380-702, Republic of Korea.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors are equally contributed to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Innovative methods to detect and kill pathogenic bacteria have a pivotal role in the eradication of infectious diseases and the prevention of the growth of antibiotic-resistant bacteria. The combination of fluorescent carbon dots (FCDs) with silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) is an effective material for synergic detection and antimicrobial activity determination. However, the fluorescence quenching of the FCDs owing to an interaction with AgNP is a major limitation. In this study, we designed a system to utilize poly(vinylpyrrolidone) (PVP) and catechol chemistry (PVP@Ag:FCD) in order to avoid the fluorescence quenching of the FCD-AgNP combination due to Forster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET). PVP@Ag:FCD exhibited bright fluorescence, which can be used for bacterial detection, through the promotion of electrostatic binding with the negatively-charged bacterial surface and generation of fluorescence quenching due to aggregation-induced quenching. Furthermore, the presence of silver nanoparticles in PVP@Ag:FCD produced an excellent bacteria killing efficiency against E. coli and S. aureus, even at low concentrations (0.1 mg/mL). In contaminated river water, the PVP@Ag:FCD system showed a simple, highly sensitive, and effective performance for both the detection and eradication of bacteria. Therefore, this system offers an auspicious method for the future detection and killing of bacteria.

ce:section.ce:para: Pathogenic bacteria have emerged as major concern for human health, because they can cause infectious diseases spread by water, air, or direct contact [1,2]. The detection and eradication of bacteria play a vital role in addressing this challenge, particularly in the field of medical products, public health, food processing, and water purification [3–5]. Various methods have been applied for bacterial detection, including polymerase chain reaction (PCR), specimen culture, and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [6,7]. However, these laboratory-based techniques are time consuming, high cost, and require specific instruments and trained users [8,9]. From an antibacterial perspective, a significant increase in antibiotic-resistant bacteria has reached a critical level owing to the extensive use of antibiotics [10]. Therefore, alternative methods for bacterial detection and killing are necessary to overcome those issues [11].

The development of alternative antibacterial agent for the eradication of bacterial contaminations without the induction of antibiotic resistance has recently been studied. Organic antibacterial substances, such as chlorinated phenyl esters, chlorhexidine, and organic nitrogen compounds, offer several advantages, but their instability in the environment represents the main drawbacks [12–14]. Alternatively, metal nanoparticles, such as silver, copper, zinc, and titanium, have been identified as possessing antibacterial activities; among them, silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) have shown the most promising potency as a simple, stable, and rapid antibacterial agent [15,16]. Despite their excellent antibacterial activity, studies combining these nanoparticles with bacterial detection are limited. Therefore, we believe that the usage of fluorescent dyes or other fluorescent materials for sensing in combination with AgNP as an antibacterial agent could offer a good strategy for the detection and killing of bacteria.

Various techniques were utilized to detect the presence of bacteria, such as surface plasmon resonance (SPR) [17,18]. However, its limited penetration of bacteria and less sensitivity become the major issues on applying this method [9]. On the other hand, fluorescent carbon dots (FCDs) are useful in many applications, including bioimaging, biosensing, and drug delivery, owing to their excellent photo-luminescent properties, easy to modify and functionalize, good stability, low photo-bleaching effect, and high biocompatibility [19–23]. In the field of bio-sensing, FCDs can be employed for bacterial detection utilizing their fluorescence on/off behavior with outstanding performance, easy operation, rapid results, and high sensitivity [24]. By taking advantage of the anionic surface of bacteria, the development of cationic FCDs is highly effective in the capture of bacteria and subsequently triggering aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching due to hydrophobic interaction between hydrophobic site of bacteria and FCD [25–27]. Due to these benefits, FCDs can be considered in combination with antibacterial agents such as AgNP for the development of dual-function systems for bacterial detection and eradication.

However, the interaction between fluorescent materials and metals like Ag may result in fluorescence quenching owing to the Forster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) phenomenon [28–33], which would limit the ability of FCDs to detect bacteria. Hence, we first reported a simple and sensitive bacterial detection probe in addition to an effective killing method using poly(vinylpyrrolidone) (PVP) and catechol to facilitate binding between FCD and PVP-modified AgNP (PVP@Ag:FCD) with no fluorescence quenching due to indirect interaction between FCD and AgNP [29–31]. PVP@Ag:FCD is expected to show a highly sensitive interaction with the negatively-charged bacterial surface via electrostatic interaction and generate aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching due to hydrophobic interaction between hydrophobic site of bacteria and hydrophobic chain of PVP@Ag:FCD. Furthermore, our material possessed effective antibacterial activity against gram-negative (Escherichia coli) and gram-positive (Staphylococcus aureus) owing to the presence of AgNP when used to analyze contaminated river water. Therefore, this system offered a promising method for the simple and remarkably susceptible detection and killing of bacteria by a combination of FCDs and AgNPs.

Poly(vinylpyrrolidone) (PVP, molecular weight: 40,000), ethyl bromide, 2-chloro-3′,4′-dihydroxyacetophenone (CCDP), silver nitrate (AgNO3), deionized water, ethanol, phosphate-buffered saline (PBS), nitrogen, diethylether, Luria-Bertani (LB) media, MRS media, agar, 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), and deuterium oxide (D2O) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, South Korea.

1H NMR spectra were acquired using a Bruker AVANCE 400 MHz spectrometer. The absorption spectra of sample were obtained using a UV–visible absorption spectrophotometer (Optizen 2120 UV spectrophotometer, Mecasys). Infrared spectroscopy was recorded using FTIR spectrometer (Thermoscientific, Nicolet 380). Dynamic light scattering (DLS; Zetasizer Nano, Malvern, Germany) was used to measure particle size. Surface morphology and elemental composition were characterized using a SEM/EDX (JSM-6700F, JEOL, Japan). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM; JEM-2100F, JEOL) was conducted with an 80–200 kV electron gun. X-ray diffraction spectra were observed using an XRD Bruker AXS ADVANCES D-8. XPS spectra were obtained using an Omicrometer ESCALAB (Omicrometer, Taunusstein, Germany). The fluorescence profiles were determined using a luminescence spectrometer (L550B, Perkin Elmer). Cyclic voltammetry was conducted using μStat 4000P Multi Potentiostat (DROPSENS, Spain). A confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM; LSM510 Carl Zeiss, Germany) was utilized to perform live and dead bacteria analysis with 488 and 543 nm emission filters. Flow cytometric analysis (FACS) was conducted using an Attune NxT Acoustic Focusing Cytometer.

The quaternization reaction was conducted to synthesize catechol-quaternized PVP (C-PVP). Briefly, 2 g of PVP and 0.2799 g of CCDP were dissolved and stirred at 70 °C in 150 mL of anhydrous ethanol for 12 h under nitrogen atmosphere. Subsequently, the solvent was evaporated and the product precipitated using diethyl ether then dried in vacuum oven. The resulted C-PVP polymer was carbonized by dissolving 1 g polymer with 10 mL H2SO4 (36 N) for approximately 1 min. The purification process was conducted through dialysis (molecular weight cut-off = 1000) for 24 h against water and then freeze-dried to obtain the FCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of catechol/ethyl bromide-quaternized PVP with AgNP (PVP@Ag)
ce:section.ce:para: Ethyl bromide (3.7 mL) was reacted with C-PVP (2 g) in 150 mL anhydrous ethanol at 70 °C for 24 h under nitrogen atmosphere. Subsequently, the solvent was removed using rotary evaporator, precipitated in diethyl ether, and the remaining sample was dried in vacuum oven. The produced catechol/ethyl bromide-quaternized PVP (C/E-PVP) (4 g) and AgNO3 (0.134 g) were mixed in 70 mL anhydrous ethanol and stirred at 70 °C for 6 h under nitrogen atmosphere. Next, the solvent was evaporated, and the product was precipitated using diethyl ether and dried in vacuum oven to produce PVP@Ag.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of catechol-crosslinked FCD with PVP@Ag (PVP@Ag:FCD)
ce:section.ce:para: FCD and PVP@Ag with a mass ratio of 5:1 were dissolved in 20 mL TBS pH 8.5, and reacted for 24 h at room temperature to obtain PVP@Ag:FCD. The product was purified by centrifugation and freeze drying.

Solutions of E. coli (gram-negative, strain ATCC 25922) and S. aureus (gram-positive, strain ATCC 25323) were prepared in LB and MRS broth media, respectively, and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. Subsequently, liquid media-contained bacteria were centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 10 min to remove the media and harvested the bacteria. Then, 5 mg/mL of PVP@Ag:FCD was mixed with the bacteria and incubated at 37 °C for 1 h. After incubation, the solution was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 10 min to obtain bacteria-labeled PVP@Ag:FCD precipitate, followed by washing it with PBS pH 7.4 to remove the unreacted PVP@Ag:FCD and perform re-centrifugation to obtain bacteria-labeled PVP@Ag:FCD. To check the fluorescence change after detection, the bacteria-labeled PVP@Ag:FCD was dissolved in 1 mL of PBS pH 7,4, and observed under the photoluminescence spectrometer with excitation wavelength of 400 nm and under confocal laser scanning microscope. Flow cytometry analysis was also performed by using BL1 (Blue Light) emission filter and FSC-SSC plot.

For cyclic voltammetry analysis, commercial SPCE strips were modified with PVP@Ag:FCD by a drop-coating technique. Briefly, 10 μL of the PVP@Ag:FCD (1 mg/mL) was dropped onto the SPCE working electrode surface, followed by drying it in oven at 90 °C for 10 min. Subsequently, SPCE were incubated in the bacterial solution, then washed with PBS and dried at room temperature. The modified electrode was then analyzed by using CV at a scan rate of 50 mV/s.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and anti-jamming test of PVP@Ag:FCD
ce:section.ce:para: To determine selectivity of PVP@Ag:FCD on detecting bacteria, evaluation towards potential interference such as cell, negative-charge amino acid and anionic salt was conducted. In brief, 5 mg/mL of PVP@Ag:FCD was incubated with 0.1 M of each Br−, HPO4 2−, SO4 2−, Cl−, glutamic acid, and 105 CFU/mL of MDCK cell at 37 °C for 1 h. Then, centrifugation and washing procedure were conducted with same method in bacteria detection assay. The obtained sample was checked using photoluminescence spectrometer with excitation wavelength of 400 nm. For anti-jamming test, all interferences and PVP@Ag:FCD were mixed together to obtain interference soup, and the fluorescence intensity was checked in the absence and presence of 107 CFU/mL of bacteria.

To evaluate biocompatibility of PVP@Ag:FCD, MTT assay was performed using Madin–Darby canine kidney epithelial cell (MDCK). Firstly, MDCK cell was treated with various concentrations of PVP@Ag:FCD (0.001–5 mg/mL), and MDCK cell only (105 CFU/mL) was used as a control. After incubation for 24 h at 37 °C, media was removed and 20 μL of 3-(4, 5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2, 5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) solution was added, followed by further incubation for 4 h. Lastly, 180 μL of MTT solubilizing agent was added into the cell solution and shaking for 15 min. The absorbance of sample was measured at the wavelength of 570 nm. The relative cell viability was calculated by comparing the PVP@Ag:FCD-treated MDCK with the control well containing MDCK cell only.

The colloidal stability test of PVP@Ag:FCD was performed by storing PVP@Ag:FCD for 72 h, followed by checking the particle size using DLS spectrometer and UV–vis absorbance at 350 nm using UV–vis spectrometer.

The E. coli and S. aureus solutions (107 CFU/mL) were cultured in incubator for 24 h at 37 °C with various concentrations of PVP@Ag:FCD. Subsequently, the bacteria were spread into petri dishes to evaluate the growth inhibition. The antibacterial activity was evaluated as a depletion in cell units after re-incubation for 24 h at 37 °C. The control comprised both types of bacteria in the absence of PVP@Ag:FCD.

The phenomenon of fluorescence quenching is a major concern for the combination of fluorescent materials with metals. As illustrated in Fig. 1a, metals such as Au and Ag, induce the fluorescence quenching of fluorescent material through the FRET phenomenon [28–33]. In this study, we developed a system that was able to overcome quenching problems when combining fluorescence material with metal nanoparticles. PVP-catechol was employed to modify the surface of AgNP before combining with fluorescence material such as FCD. This concept can inhibit FRET phenomenon due to inhibition of direct interaction and increase the distance between AgNP and FCD, hence, this approach can minimize the FRET phenomenon and can be applied for fluorescence-based bacterial detection and eradication. As shown in Fig. 1b, we designed PVP@Ag:FCD through catechol chemistry; at basic pH, the catechol moieties are cross-linked [34–36]. First, we synthesized FCD from catechol-quaternized PVP backbone (C-PVP) via a carbonization method. Meanwhile, PVP@Ag was synthesized via the reduction of AgNO3 to AgNP by the catechol/ethyl bromide-quaternized PVP backbone (C/E-PVP), since the catechol moieties acted as reducing agent for AgNO3 to form AgNP [37]. In addition, the quaternization of PVP backbone using ethyl bromide provided sufficient positive charge, owing to the formation of quaternary amines in the PVP structure, for interaction with the negative charge of the bacterial cell wall. Finally, FCD and PVP@Ag were mixed in TBS pH 8.5 to achieve cross-linking between the catechol moieties on each surface and produce the highly-fluorescent PVP@Ag:FCD. The positive charge of PVP@Ag:FCD promoted binding with the negatively-charged bacterial surface and provided detection ability via aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching, whereas the presence of AgNP conferred antibacterial activity.

The structure of PVP@Ag:FCD was characterized using 1H NMR, UV–vis, and FTIR spectrometer. As shown in Fig. 2a, the peak at 1.1 and 3.5 ppm represented the ethyl substituent of the quaternary amine that originated from ethyl bromide. The quaternary amines in PVP@Ag:FCD provide a positive charge that induces binding to the negatively-charged bacterial surface, which is a vital component of bacteria detection. 1H NMR also revealed the presence of catechol moieties on C/E-PVP, FCD, and PVP@Ag:FCD, with the catechol peak observed between 6 and 8 ppm. This was also confirmed by the UV–vis absorption analysis, which showed the prominent absorption spectra at 260 and 350 nm for all samples; this reflected the aromatic π-π transition of catechol (Fig. 2b). Furthermore, the absorbance at 420 nm was referred the typical SPR peak of AgNP in PVP@Ag and PVP@Ag:FCD, which revealed the presence of AgNP in the PVP@Ag and PVP@Ag:FCD. The FT-IR transmittance (Fig. S1) showed CH stretching at 2900 cm−1, COOH stretching at 1150 cm−1, CO stretching at about 1680–1740 cm−1, and OH stretching at about 3630 cm−1, which indicating PVP-catechol and FCD. The fluorescence properties of FCD were characterized using PL spectrometer. as shown in Fig. 2c, FCD exhibited strong fluorescence emissions, particularly at an excitation wavelength of 380 nm. Through the exploitation of the catechol properties in basic condition (pH 8.5), FCD and PVP@Ag can be conjugated through catechol cross-linking to produce PVP@Ag:FCD. To evaluate the fluorescence behavior after the combination of FCD and AgNP, we examined the fluorescence intensity of PVP@Ag:FCD using PL spectrometer. After cross-linking, the maximum emission wavelength was shifted to an excitation wavelength of 400 nm (Figs. 2d, S2) due to the presence of AgNP. This was might be because the slight interaction between surface plasmon resonances of AgNP and fluorophores of FCD was occurred and affected the fluorescence emission of FCD [38,39]. Furthermore, PVP@Ag:FCD exhibited high fluorescence intensity like FCD and showed no fluorescence quenching. This was due to increasing distance and indirect interaction between FCD and AgNP [29–31], which caused by the modification of AgNP surface with PVP-catechol. PVP-catechol can hinder energy transfer between surface plasmon resonance of Ag and FCD, and preventing FRET fluorescence quenching. This result indicates that our system was successfully maintain the fluorescence intensity of PVP@Ag:FCD and can be used for further fluorescence-based bacteria detection.

The DLS results revealed the average size distribution of FCD, PVP@Ag, and PVP@Ag:FCD in the aqueous state. As shown in Fig. 3a, the sizes were 14.1 nm for FCD, 102.4 nm for PVP@Ag, and 114.5 nm for PVP@Ag:FCD. The larger size of PVP@Ag:FCD than FCD or PVP@Ag resulted from the successful catechol cross-linking of FCD and PVP@Ag. Those particle sizes further were confirmed by SEM (Fig. 3b), which showed comparable results to the DLS measurement. TEM analysis also can observe the size and shape of nanoparticles. As shown in Fig. 3c, the shape of the PVP@Ag:FCD nanoparticles was spherical and a similar size to previous measurement methods (approximately 115 nm). To confirm the presence of AgNP in PVP@Ag:FCD, EDX analysis was performed (Fig. 3b) and it can be confirmed that 1.08% of Ag was present in the composite. XPS spectra also revealed the presence of the FCD (Fig. S3a) and AgNP (Fig. S3b), indicated by peak at 284.0 eV and 287.5 eV which attributed to CC and CO of FCD, also peak at 368.2 eV and 374.6 eV which attributed to Ag 3d5/2 and Ag 3d3/2. Furthermore, high resolution TEM, XRD, and SAED were used to observe the lattice structure and diffraction pattern of PVP@Ag:FCD. TEM images (Fig. 3c) showed the lattice structure of Ag (0.23–0.25 nm) along with typical lattice structure of FCD (0.32–0.34 nm) in the PVP@Ag:FCD, which indicated the presence of FCD and AgNP in the composite. Moreover, XRD pattern (Fig. S4) revealed that typical diffraction pattern of FCD (002) and AgNP (111) was appeared at 2θ of 22o and 38o, respectively. These TEM and XRD result were strongly correlated with the diffraction pattern observed by SAED that showed diffraction pattern of FCD (002) with d-spacing of 0.23 nm and AgNP (111) with d-spacing 0.34 nm (Fig. S5). Based on these data, it can be confirmed that FCD and AgNP were present and successfully combined in PVP@Ag:FCD.

One approach that has been used for bacteria detection is the employment of electrostatic interaction between the cationic sites of the material and the negatively-charged bacteria surface, followed by aggregation between hydrophobic site of bacteria and hydrophobic segment of PVP@Ag:FCD which cause aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching [25–27]. This fluorescence-based bacteria detection was evaluated using PL spectrophotometry at excitation wavelength of 400 nm, and compared the PL intensity before and after interaction (Fig. 4 ). Firstly, bacteria detection was conducted by varying the concentrations of E. coli and S. aureus. As shown in Fig. 4a and b, after incubation for 1 h at 37 °C with E. coli and S. aureus, the PVP@Ag:FCD fluorescence intensity significantly decreased in the concentration range of 101–107 CFU/mL, compared with only PVP@Ag:FCD as a control (5 mg/mL). This result demonstrated the fluorescence quenching effect due to interaction between the PVP@Ag:FCD and bacteria. Moreover, this PVP@Ag:FCD can detect the presence of bacteria in small concentration up to 101 CFU/mL, revealing the sensitivity of this biosensor. The bacteria detection performance was also assessed by varying concentration of PVP@Ag:FCD (0.1–10 mg/mL) which treated with 107 CFU/mL of bacteria solution, and compared with only PVP@Ag:FCD solution (5 mg/mL) as a control. As can be seen in Fig. 4c and d, the PVP@Ag:FCD exhibited dramatic decreased after bacteria detection in all concentration. Based on these results, the PVP@Ag:FCD was able to sensitively detect the bacteria due to electrostatic interaction between cationic site of material and negative charge of bacterial surface. From these results, we can see also that greater quenching occurred in E. coli than S. aureus. This was related to the different chemical components in the outer membrane structures between gram-negative and gram-positive bacteria. The electrostatic bonds in PVP@Ag:FCD with the phospholipid and phosphate groups of the E. coli outer membrane were known to be stronger than the interaction that occurs with the long chains of teichoic acid in the S. aureus cell wall [40], thus generating higher detection efficiency in E. coli. Further observation about fluorescence changes in bacteria detection was conducted using confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM). As demonstrated in Fig. 4e, the fluorescence change of PVP@Ag:FCD from bright green emission before incubation to no emission after incubation with either E. coli or S. aureus, confirmed that bacteria detection based on fluorescence quenching can be applied. To evaluate the selectivity of PVP@Ag:FCD on detecting bacteria, the quenching effect towards potential interference such as cell (MDCK), negative-charge amino acid (glutamic acid) and anionic salt (Br−, HPO4 2−, SO4 2−, Cl−) was conducted. As can be seen in Fig. S6, the PVP@Ag:FCD demonstrated high selectivity towards E. coli and S. aureus without resulting significant fluorescence quenching towards other interferences. Moreover, the PVP@Ag:FCD also showed no significant response towards interference soup, while the fluorescence of PVP@Ag:FCD was dramatically quenched when treated with soup containing bacteria. Thus, these results indicated that the PVP@Ag:FCD have excellent selectivity towards bacteria and good anti-jamming capability. The selectivity of PVP@Ag:FCD on detecting bacteria was due to electrostatic interaction between PVP@Ag:FCD and negatively-charged of bacterial surface, and followed by aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching due to aggregation between hydrophobic site of bacteria and hydrophobic segment of PVP@Ag:FCD.

Furthermore, SEM imaging was used to examine the condition of the bacterial cell wall. As shown in Fig. 5a, the bacterial surface became rough compared with control owing to the adherence of PVP@Ag:FCD onto the bacterial cell wall. This indicated that there was an interaction between the cationic sides of PVP@Ag:FCD with the negative charge of bacterial surface. To confirm the binding phenomenon of PVP@Ag:FCD with bacteria, cyclic voltammetry (CV) analysis was performed (Fig. 5b). By measuring the change of electrical current signal as the effect of electron transfer inhibition when bacteria bonded with PVP@Ag:FCD, the interaction between PVP@Ag:FCD and bacteria can be validated [41]. As can be seen in Fig. 5b, there was a decreased in current intensity observed by cyclic voltammogram for PVP@Ag:FCD after binding with bacteria. This was occurred due to inhibition of electron transfer from PVP@Ag:FCD to electrode as a result of interference from bacteria which bonded with PVP@Ag:FCD. This reflected that the electrostatic interaction was occurred between PVP@Ag:FCD and bacteria, and affected the amount of current signal. Flow cytometric analysis was also performed to understand the change in fluorescence and to support the PL and confocal image data (Fig. 5c). The plot of the forward scatter count (FSC) against the side scatter count (SSC) illustrated the size and granularity of the bacterial cells. After PVP@Ag:FCD bound to bacteria, the slope of the scattered dot plot was altered due to the increase of granularity caused by the attachment of PVP@Ag:FCD on to the bacteria surface. Furthermore, the fluorescence emission change can be detected and counted as a fluorescence intensity mean value. As shown in the histogram, PVP@Ag:FCD had a higher emission (mean value: 148) than only PVP@Ag (mean value: 113) and C-PVP before carbonization (mean value: 110). This revealed that the presence of FCD enhanced the fluorescence intensity in PVP@Ag:FCD and not in PVP@Ag. For bacteria detection, the fluorescence mean value of E. coli and S. aureus as a control was 124 and 136, respectively. On the other hand, the mean value of PVP@Ag:FCD was decreased from 148 to 124 for E. coli and from 148 to 135 for S. aureus, which were notably same value as a control. These results indicated that the typical fluorescence of PVP@Ag:FCD was dramatically decrease after incubated with bacteria, and these results were in a good agreement with dramatic fluorescence quenching on PL data.

The antibacterial activity of PVP@Ag:FCD against gram-negative and gram-positive bacteria was examined via a colony plating method. E. coli and S. aureus solution (107 CFU/mL) was incubated with various concentrations of PVP@Ag:FCD at 37 °C for 24 h, then the bacteria were spread onto LB (E. coli) and MRS (S. aureus) solid media for re-incubation. The control, a bacteria-only solution, was prepared using the same procedure. First, we compared antibacterial activity between PVP@Ag and FCD at same concentration (10 mg/mL) with same procedure as before. As shown in Fig. 6a, E. coli and S. aureus treated with FCD grew abundantly, but did not grow in the presence of PVP@Ag. From this evidence, AgNP was the main factor responsible for the antibacterial ability of PVP@Ag:FCD. Many studies and reports have already stated that AgNP is effective for bacterial eradication through damage the outer membrane of the bacterial cell, which triggers the disruption of metabolic processes. AgNP will accumulate in the bacterial membrane and aggregation occurs when the bacteria treatment is applied; thus, this condition disrupts membrane integrity and causes bacterial cell death. Moreover, their high activity at low concentrations against a broad range of bacteria makes this material more efficient than other metals [42]. Further experiments were conducted to observe antibacterial activity of PVP@Ag:FCD at different material concentrations. An increased PVP@Ag:FCD concentration resulted in lower colony growth, which implied that a higher concentration was related to more effective antibacterial activity. Furthermore, PVP@Ag:FCD exhibited excellent bacteria killing efficiency, even at low concentrations (80%–90% of bacteria killed by 0.1 mg/mL PVP@Ag:FCD), with complete killing of both E. coli and S. aureus at 5 mg/mL (Fig. 6b and c). Flow cytometry analysis was performed to confirm the live and dead bacteria using SYTO 9 and PI staining method. As can be seen in Fig. 6d, the dot distribution was changed after bacteria were treated with PVP@Ag:FCD, as the dot distribution moved from lower quadrant (live/SYTO 9 staining) into upper quadrant (dead/PI staining). It can be observed that in the presence of PVP@Ag:FCD, E. coli & S. aureus viability was decreased to almost 100%. These results demonstrated the ability of PVP@Ag:FCD as an effective and efficient antibacterial agent.

To investigate the cytotoxicity of PVP@Ag:FCD, MTT assay was performed using MDCK cell treated with various concentrations of PVP@Ag:FCD (0.001–5 mg/mL). The result showed that PVP@Ag:FCD was non-toxic towards MDCK cell with cell viability around 88.20%–99.74% in the range concentration of 0.001 mg/mL up to 5 mg/mL (Fig. S7), which indicated that this material has excellent biocompatibility. This biocompatibility was understandably due to the presence of PVP and FCD which deposited on the surface of the AgNP. Furthermore, the deposited PVP and FCD on the AgNP surface provided colloidal stability on the PVP@Ag:FCD as shown in Fig. S8. After 72 h, PVP@Ag:FCD showed colloidal stability under UV–Vis observation and there was no significant change on particle size. From these results, PVP@Ag:FCD provides excellent antibacterial activity with excellent stability and biocompatibility towards MDCK cell.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bacteria detection and antibacterial performance of PVP@Ag:FCD in “real-world” samples
ce:section.ce:para: To check the material performance for bacteria detection in environments with various matrices, we tested PVP@Ag:FCD in “real-world” samples. We sampled contaminated water from one of the rivers in Chungju City, South Korea, and tested our material for detecting bacteria. First, we checked the presence of bacteria in contaminated water by culturing in solid media. The sample was cultured on LB and MRS media at 37 °C for 24 h incubation time. After confirmation of the availability of bacteria, bacteria detection was attempted by adding PVP@Ag:FCD into contaminated river water using similar method previously performed at 37 °C, with 1 h incubation, and evaluated using confocal laser scanning microscopy. Bacterial detection in distillated water was also conducted as a control. As shown in Fig. 7a, confocal images exhibited no change in emission color when PVP@Ag:FCD was introduced into distillated water, which indicated the presence of no significant amount of bacteria. Conversely, there was a remarkable alteration in fluorescence intensity when contaminated water was treated with PVP@Ag:FCD (Fig. 7b). Those images showed the fluorescence quenching from bright green to no emission, which indicated that PVP@Ag:FCD had the ability to detect the presence of bacteria in real-world sample. We also examined the antibacterial activity of PVP@Ag:FCD in contaminated river water using the colony plating method. As shown in Fig. 7b, the growth of bacteria from contaminated water was inhibited by PVP@Ag:FCD in both LB and MRS media. All these data proved that the combination of FCD and AgNP via catechol chemistry can be applied for the synergic detection and killing of bacteria in a natural environment; furthermore, it represents a promising dual-functional material, which is simple, sensitive, and highly effective, for future use in bacteria detection and eradication.

In summary, we successfully synthesized a dual-functional material based on FCDs and AgNPs (PVP@Ag:FCD), which acted as a simple, sensitive, and highly effective material for the detection and killing of gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria. PVP@Ag:FCD, which was synthesized from PVP polymer via catechol chemistry, exhibited strong fluorescence intensity which demonstrated that our approach avoided the FRET fluorescence quenching of FCD in the presence of AgNP. The fluorescence profile of bacteria treated with PVP@Ag:FCD demonstrated remarkable change owing to electrostatic interactions between the cationic sites of PVP@Ag:FCD and the negatively-charged bacterial surface, which resulted in quenching as the fluorescence emission diminished. Moreover, the presence of AgNP in PVP@Ag:FCD generated a great bacteria killing efficiency against both E. coli and S. aureus, even at low concentrations. When applied into real-world samples, PVP@Ag:FCD also exhibited excellent performance for the detection and eradication of bacteria in contaminated river water, which indicated this method could be used in an environmental matrix. Thus, this approach can be a promising strategy to combine biosensors with antibacterial activity for a simple, sensitive, and effective method of bacteria detection and eradication.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by Grant No. 10062079 from the Ministry of Trade, Industry and Energy (MOTIE) and the Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education (NRF-2017R1A2B2002365 and 2018R1A6A1A03023788).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.msec.2018.12.070.
